nodes.name: World Languages
nodes.description: Major languages, language families, and writing systems

items.prompt: What language has the most native speakers in the world, with over 900 million?
items.explanation:
Over 900 million native speakers, primarily in China, Taiwan, and Singapore
Official language of the People's Republic of China (as Pǔtōnghuà, 'common speech') and Taiwan (as Guóyǔ, 'national language')
A tonal language with four main tones plus a neutral tone; the same syllable spoken in different tones has entirely different meanings
Written using Chinese characters (hanzi); simplified characters used in mainland China, traditional in Taiwan and Hong Kong
Belongs to the Sino-Tibetan language family
One of the six official languages of the United Nations
items.cardFront: Language with the most native speakers worldwide

items.prompt: What language is the most widely spoken in the world when counting both native and non-native speakers, with over 1.4 billion total?
items.explanation:
Approximately 380 million native speakers, but over 1.4 billion total speakers including L2
Official or co-official language in over 60 countries across every inhabited continent
A West Germanic language heavily influenced by Norman French after the 1066 conquest
The global lingua franca for science, aviation, computing, diplomacy, and international business
Has the largest vocabulary of any language, with over 170,000 words in current use per the Oxford English Dictionary
The Great Vowel Shift (1400–1700) is why English spelling is so inconsistent with pronunciation

items.prompt: What is the world's largest language family, containing English, Hindi, Spanish, Russian, and most European languages?
items.explanation:
Spoken by about 3.2 billion people — roughly 46% of the world's population
Includes branches: Germanic, Romance, Slavic, Indo-Iranian, Celtic, Hellenic, Baltic, Albanian, Armenian
Sir William Jones first proposed the family in 1786, noting similarities between Sanskrit, Latin, and Greek
The hypothetical common ancestor, Proto-Indo-European, was likely spoken around 4500–2500 BC
The Kurgan hypothesis places the homeland in the Pontic–Caspian steppe (modern Ukraine/southern Russia)
Notable non-Indo-European languages in Europe: Finnish, Hungarian, Estonian (Uralic), and Basque (isolate)
items.cardFront: World's largest language family by number of speakers

items.prompt: What language family, the world's second-largest by number of speakers, contains Chinese, Burmese, and Tibetan?
items.explanation:
Second-largest language family by number of speakers, after Indo-European
Contains roughly 400–500 languages across East Asia, Southeast Asia, and the Himalayas
Two major branches: Sinitic (Chinese languages) and Tibeto-Burman
Most Sino-Tibetan languages are tonal, using pitch to distinguish word meaning
Chinese varieties alone account for the vast majority of the family's speakers
The family's internal classification is still debated; some linguists propose additional branches
items.cardFront: Second-largest language family (contains Chinese and Tibetan)

items.prompt: What alphabet, developed in the 9th century for Slavic languages, is used to write Russian, Ukrainian, Bulgarian, and Serbian?
items.explanation:
Developed in the First Bulgarian Empire in the 9th–10th century, based on Greek uncial script
Named after Saint Cyril (827–869), though actually created by his disciples; Cyril and his brother Methodius developed the earlier Glagolitic alphabet
Used by over 250 million people across roughly 50 languages
The modern Russian Cyrillic alphabet has 33 letters; other languages add or remove characters
Peter the Great reformed the Russian Cyrillic in 1708, creating a more Westernized 'civil script'
Adopted well beyond Slavic languages: Kazakh, Mongolian, Tajik, and many languages of the former Soviet Union
items.cardFront: Script used for Russian, Bulgarian, and Serbian

items.prompt: What writing system, written right-to-left, is the second most widely used alphabet in the world after Latin?
items.explanation:
Written right-to-left; the second most widely used script in the world after Latin
Used for Arabic, Persian (Farsi), Urdu, Pashto, Kurdish, and historically for Turkish (until 1928) and Malay (Jawi)
28 letters in the Arabic alphabet; letters change form depending on position in a word (initial, medial, final, isolated)
Evolved from the Nabataean alphabet, itself derived from Aramaic
Arabic calligraphy is considered a major art form in Islamic culture; geometric and floral because of aniconism
The Quran's status as the literal word of God in Arabic has kept the classical language stable for over 1,400 years
items.cardFront: Second most widely used script worldwide, written right-to-left

items.prompt: What script, whose name means 'of the divine city,' is used to write Hindi, Sanskrit, Marathi, and Nepali?
items.explanation:
Name derives from deva ('deity') + nāgarī ('urban, of the city'), loosely 'script of the divine city'
An abugida (alphasyllabary): each consonant carries an inherent vowel that can be modified with diacritical marks
Used for Hindi, Sanskrit, Marathi, Nepali, and several other South Asian languages
Written left-to-right with a distinctive horizontal line (shīrōrekhā) connecting letters along the top
Developed from the Brahmi script (3rd century BC) via the Gupta and Nāgarī scripts
The Unicode standard encodes Devanagari in the range U+0900 to U+097F
items.cardFront: Script used for Hindi, Sanskrit, and Nepali

items.prompt: What is the most widely spoken constructed language, created in 1887 by L. L. Zamenhof to promote international communication?
items.explanation:
Created by L. L. Zamenhof, a Polish-Jewish ophthalmologist, published in 1887 as Lingvo Internacia
Zamenhof published under the pseudonym 'Doktoro Esperanto' ('Doctor Hopeful'), which became the language's name
Estimated 100,000–2,000,000 speakers worldwide; exact numbers are disputed
Designed for simplicity: 16 grammar rules, no exceptions, phonetic spelling, regular verb conjugation
Has a native speaker community (denaskaj esperantistoj) — children raised with Esperanto as a first language
The only constructed language with its own Wikipedia (over 300,000 articles) and Google Translate support

items.prompt: What ancient language served as the lingua franca of Western scholarship and the Catholic Church for over a millennium, and is the ancestor of the Romance languages?
items.explanation:
Originally the language of Latium (the region around Rome); spread across Europe with Roman conquest
Classical Latin (1st century BC–2nd century AD) is the literary standard; Vulgar Latin was the everyday spoken form
Vulgar Latin evolved into the Romance languages: Spanish, Portuguese, French, Italian, Romanian
Remained the language of science, law, medicine, and theology in Europe well into the 18th century
Newton's Principia Mathematica (1687) and Linnaeus's Systema Naturae (1735) were written in Latin
Still the official language of the Vatican and the Roman Catholic Church
items.cardFront: Ancestor of the Romance languages; lingua franca of medieval Europe

items.prompt: What Bantu language of South Africa is famous for its use of click consonants, and was the native language of Nelson Mandela?
items.explanation:
A Bantu language spoken by about 8 million people, primarily in South Africa's Eastern Cape
Features 18 click consonants, borrowed from the neighboring Khoisan languages through centuries of contact
Click sounds are represented in writing by the letters c (dental click), q (alveolar click), and x (lateral click)
Nelson Mandela and Desmond Tutu were both native Xhosa speakers
One of South Africa's 11 official languages; the second most common home language after Zulu
Miriam Makeba's 'Click Song' (Qongqothwane) introduced Xhosa clicks to a global audience in the 1960s
items.cardFront: Bantu language with click consonants; Mandela's native tongue

items.prompt: What language, spoken in parts of Spain and France, is Europe's only surviving language isolate with no known relatives?
items.explanation:
A language isolate: no demonstrated genetic relationship to any other living language
Spoken by about 750,000 people in the Basque Country spanning northeastern Spain and southwestern France
Predates the arrival of Indo-European languages in Europe; possibly a remnant of pre-Indo-European languages
The Basque name for the language is Euskara; the people call themselves Euskaldunak
An ergative–absolutive language, a rare typological feature among European languages
Survived Roman, Visigothic, Frankish, and Spanish/French cultural domination for millennia

items.prompt: What ancient Indo-European language is the classical literary language of Hinduism and the liturgical language of Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain traditions?
items.explanation:
The classical language of ancient India; the oldest attested form is Vedic Sanskrit (c. 1500–1200 BC)
The Rigveda, composed in Vedic Sanskrit, is one of the oldest surviving texts in any Indo-European language
Pāṇini's Aṣṭādhyāyī (c. 4th century BC) is the earliest known formal grammar of any language
Sanskrit's systematic grammar deeply influenced modern linguistics; Noam Chomsky acknowledged Pāṇini's influence
The word 'Sanskrit' means 'refined' or 'perfected' (saṃskṛta)
Source of many English loanwords: avatar, karma, yoga, nirvana, mantra, jungle, guru
items.cardFront: Classical literary and liturgical language of ancient India

items.prompt: What artifact, discovered in 1799 during Napoleon's Egyptian campaign, was the key to deciphering Egyptian hieroglyphics?
items.explanation:
Discovered in July 1799 by French soldiers near the town of Rashid (Rosetta) in the Nile Delta
A granodiorite stele inscribed with the same decree in three scripts: hieroglyphic, Demotic, and Greek
The decree (196 BC) was issued by priests at Memphis affirming the royal cult of Ptolemy V Epiphanes
Jean-François Champollion used the stone to crack hieroglyphics in 1822, recognizing that the script was partly phonetic
Britain acquired it from France under the 1801 Treaty of Alexandria; it has been in the British Museum since 1802
Egypt has repeatedly requested its return; the phrase 'Rosetta Stone' now means any key to a difficult problem

items.prompt: What tactile writing system, invented by a 15-year-old Frenchman in 1824, uses raised dots to represent letters?
items.explanation:
Invented by Louis Braille (1809–1852), who was blinded in an accident at age 3
Based on a military 'night writing' system by Charles Barbier, designed for soldiers to read in the dark
Uses a 6-dot cell (3 rows × 2 columns) capable of 64 combinations including blank
Braille devised the system at age 15 while a student at the Royal Institute for Blind Youth in Paris
Not widely adopted during his lifetime; France officially adopted it for education in 1854, two years after his death
Adapted for virtually every language worldwide; also exists for music notation and mathematics
items.cardFront: Tactile writing system using raised dots

items.prompt: What visual language, used by over 500,000 people in the U.S. and parts of Canada, was developed at a Hartford school in the 1810s?
items.explanation:
Developed from French Sign Language (LSF), brought to the U.S. by Laurent Clerc and Thomas Hopkins Gallaudet
The American School for the Deaf, founded in Hartford, Connecticut, in 1817, was the first permanent school for deaf students in the U.S.
ASL is not a signed version of English — it has its own grammar, syntax, and idioms entirely distinct from English
Not mutually intelligible with British Sign Language (BSL), despite both countries speaking English
Gallaudet University in Washington, D.C. (founded 1864) is the world's only university designed for deaf and hard-of-hearing students
Recognized as a natural language by linguists since William Stokoe's 1960 research at Gallaudet
items.cardFront: Visual language used by deaf communities in the U.S.
